source.text: Eisenbud, Grayson, Stillman, Sturmfels — Computations in Algebraic Geometry with Macaulay 2 (Springer, 2002), Preface examples pp. vii–ix

basic_description: Defines a quotient ring R = QQ[x,y,z]/(x^3 - y^3 - z^3), then expands (x+y+z)^3 inside it. This is the opening worked example in the official Macaulay2 book preface, demonstrating quotient ring definition and polynomial arithmetic.
prompt: In Macaulay2, define the quotient ring R = QQ[x,y,z]/(x^3 - y^3 - z^3). Then compute and display the expansion of (x + y + z)^3 in this ring.

source.text: Eisenbud, Grayson, Stillman, Sturmfels — Computations in Algebraic Geometry with Macaulay 2 (Springer, 2002), Preface pp. viii–ix

basic_description: Constructs two matrices b = vars R and c = {{x^2,y^2,z^2}} over the quotient ring R = QQ[x,y,z]/(x^3-y^3-z^3), then computes the kernel of c. This is a preface example from the official Macaulay2 book showing module construction and kernel computation.
prompt: In Macaulay2, define R = QQ[x,y,z]/(x^3 - y^3 - z^3). Then define the matrix c = matrix {{x^2, y^2, z^2}} over R and compute ker c. Print the result.

source.text: Official Macaulay2 Documentation — PrimaryDecomposition package, primaryDecomposition function reference page

basic_description: Computes the primary decomposition of the ideal of 2x2 permanents of a generic 3x3 matrix over QQ[a..i], then checks that the intersection of the components equals the original ideal. Drawn directly from the official Macaulay2 PrimaryDecomposition package documentation example.
prompt: In Macaulay2, define R = QQ[a..i] and let I be the ideal of 2x2 permanents of the generic 3x3 matrix genericMatrix(R,a,3,3) (use the permanents function). Compute C = primaryDecomposition I. Then check that intersect C == I and print the number of components using #C.

source.text: Michałek & Sturmfels — Invitation to Nonlinear Algebra (AMS, 2021), Chapter 1, Section 1.1, Equation (1.1) and surrounding discussion pp. 2–3

basic_description: Defines the cubic polynomial f = det([[1,x,y],[x,1,z],[y,z,1]]) = 2xyz - x^2 - y^2 - z^2 + 1 from the opening example of Invitation to Nonlinear Algebra. Computes its four singular points by finding the variety of f and its three partial derivatives, i.e. solving the gradient system using a Groebner basis with elimination order.
prompt: In Macaulay2, define R = QQ[x,y,z] and f = 2*x*y*z - x^2 - y^2 - z^2 + 1 (the cubic from the Cayley nodal cubic surface). Define I as the ideal generated by f and its three partial derivatives diff(x,f), diff(y,f), diff(z,f). Compute and print the primary decomposition of I to find the four singular points.

source.text: Schenck — Computational Algebraic Geometry (Cambridge University Press, 2003), Exercise 1.3.1 (Decomposition I), p. 13

basic_description: Schenck Exercise 1.3.1 asks to verify that the ideal <x^2-4, y^2-1> in QQ[x,y] decomposes as an intersection of four maximal ideals corresponding to the four points {+/-2} x {+/-1}. This problem asks for a loop that generates those four maximal ideals and verifies the intersection equals the original ideal.
prompt: In Macaulay2, define R = QQ[x,y] and I = ideal(x^2 - 4, y^2 - 1). Use primaryDecomposition to decompose I, then write a scan loop that prints each component. Finally verify that intersect(primaryDecomposition I) == I and print the result.
